responseSet.title: Concept and Percept Formation in Object Relations Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Concept and Percept Formation in Object Relations Theory

J. Meloy PHD

J. Meloy

La Jolla, CA

J. Meloy  

A theoretical model is presented which differentiates between concept and percept formation in object relations theory. The model is composed of ten “units of experience” that are derived from the psychological variables of identity, space, and time. Its construct validity in relation to current psychoanalytic theory is discussed.

responseSet.title: Orality and Depression: An Empirical Study
responseSet.authorMast: Robert F. Bornstein,  Frederick G. Poynton and  Joseph Masling
responseSet.authorCitation: Bornstein, R. F., Poynton, F. G. & Masling, J.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Orality and Depression

An Empirical Study

Robert Bornstein , Frederick Poynton and Joseph Masling PHD

Joseph Masling

Joseph Masling  

Despite much theoretical speculation and some indirect evidence, no study has empirically assessed the relationship of orality to depression. In the present investigation, two separate samples of male colleges students (N1 = 276; N2 = 141) completed both the Rorschach test and the Depression Experiences Questionnaire (DEQ). More DEQ items correlated significantly with a Rorschach orality measure than would be expected by chance, but the magnitude of these correlations was small. Item analysis suggests that dependency is less a factor in depression than is a personality constellation marked by egocentrism, immaturity, fear of rejection, helplessness, and lack of integration. The results of this study are limited by its use of nonclinically disturbed male subjects.

responseSet.title: The Resentment Process: Videotape Studies
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Resentment Process

Videotape Studies

Suzanne Retzinger MA

Suzanne Retzinger

Suzanne Retzinger  

A relationship between laughter and resentment is proposed. Resentment has two components: anger (arising from an injustice/injury to self) and shame, an inhibiting factor. Resentment is defined as shame and anger occurring simultaneously. It is hypothesized that laughter dispels shame, allowing the anger to dissipate, thus resolving the resentment. Social, psychological, and biological aspects of the process are described.

Videotaped recordings of persons discussing resentful situations were analyzed for verbal and facial indications of anger. Six interviews were used; in three of them subjects laughed, but in the other three there was no laughter. It was hypothesized that following triumphant laughter, expressions of anger will decrease and authentic smiling increase. Following laughter, there was a substantial decrease in the amount of anger expressed, as well as an increase in authentic smiling. Triumphant laughter seems to signal the resolution of resentment. For the subjects who did not laugh, no change occurred in anger expressions, and authentic smiling decreased. An unexpected finding involves asymptote as a possible mechanism of mood.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Continuity and Change in Art: The Development of Modes of Representation

Sidney J. Blatt in collaboration with Ethel Blatt. Hillsdale, NJ and London: Lawrence Erlbaum Associates, Inc., 1984

Erika Fromm PHD

Erika Fromm

Erika Fromm  

Sidney and Ethel Blatt's book is a monumental, awe-inspiring, and profoundly scientific work of great inner beauty. It explores the history of painting from the Paleolithic Period (15,000–10,000, B. C.) to Contemporary Art. With enormous erudition Blatt discusses the parallel developments in art and science for the following periods: Paleolithic, Mesolithic, Egyptian art, Greco-Roman art, the art of the Middle Ages, the Renaissance, Mannerism, the Baroque, Impressionism, and 20th-century art. He conceives of three major epochs in the history of Western civilization: from ancient times through the Middle Ages; from the Renaissance through the Baroque; and from Impressionism through contemporary times. The thesis of Blatt's book is that the development of culture in mankind is fundamentally parallel to the cognitive development of the child as described by Jean Piaget. For some periods in the development of art, he adds concepts stemming from the psychoanalytic theory of object relations. Blatt examines the development of the concepts of the form of objects and of space in Western civilization as documented by paintings ranging from the cave paintings of the Paleolithic Period to the abstract art of the 20th century.

Piaget was convinced that what he had observed about the structural development of thought in individual children was applicable not only to all children but also to the development of culture. He assumed that culture, the progress of mankind, develops along the same lines as those of the cognition of the individual. Blatt sets out to test this hypothesis by carefully analyzing the history of art and by, at each point, relating the history of art to that of the history of science, philosophy, and sometimes of music or literature of the same period.

The book is well organized. Chapter 1 deals with the form of objects and of space in the history of art, with each period's search for a “vocabulary of form,” with the psychological criteria, and with the structural analysis of such vocabularies of form.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fury on Earth: A Biography of Wilhelm Reich

Myron Sharaf. New York: St. Martin's Press, 1983, xiii + 550 pp., $24.95

Pauil Roazen PHD

Pauil Roazen

Toronto

Pauil Roazen  

Wilhelm Reich is one of the most popularly known of the many prophets to have emerged from within psychoanalysis. A steady stream of books about Reich in recent years has helped satisfy the public's legitimate curiosity about him. So far the best of them is Reich's son Peter's poetic-like memoirs, A Book of Dreams. Yet Myron Sharaf's Fury on Earth is far and away the finest book on both Reich's work and his life. It is a professional piece of scholarship that may well, until the Reich Archives are finally opened, remain definitive on the subject.

The history of Freud and his circle has by now become a scholarly industry. Because of the persistence of sectarianism, though, it is not as easy as it should be to get an education in the growth of the tradition of depth psychology. Entirely aside from the continuing fascination with the founder of psychoanalysis, biographies of his followers—and the “heretics”—are steadily being published. Within the last year or so, Bertin's Marie Bonaparte (1982), Brome's Ernest Jones (1982), and Burnham's Jelliffe (1983) have appeared. The most sophisticated biography of the lot was one by Perry about the eccentric and original Harry Stack Sullivan, who had an immense impact on American psychiatry and social science. Otto Rank has also been reassessed by Menaker (1982), and evaluated in a fine biography by James Lieberman (1985).

Requests for reprints should be sent to Paul Roazen, Ph. D., 141 Chester Avenue, Toronto, Ontario M4K 2Z8, Canada.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Object Relations in Psychoanalytic Theory

Jay R. Greenberg and Stephen A. Mitchell. Cambridge, MA, and London: Harvard University Press, 1983, x + 437 pp., $24.95.

Robert Bornstein

Robert Bornstein

Robert Bornstein  

Object relations theory alters the focus of psychoanalysis, shifting the theoretical emphasis from the study of Oedipal dynamics to the phenomenon of infantile dependency as a causal factor in personality development. Although the growth of object relations theory reflects a general trend in psychoanalysis away from biologically centered instinct theory to a more interpersonal “nurture-centered” approach, the object relations perspective also has roots in a number of other areas. Theories of object relations reflect an understanding of cognitive development and the acquisition of object constancy as discussed in the writings of Piaget. Considerations arising out of the analyses of narcissistic and borderline patients-in which preoedipal reconstruction replaced Oedipal interpretation as the primary focus of treatment-also provided an impetus for the development of object relations theories of personality (Kernberg, 1970, 1975; Kohut, 1971, 1977); issues of infantile dependency and attachment became particularly salient within this framework. Ferenczi's studies of infant behavior, as well as Anna Freud's and Mahler's writings on ego development and childhood emotional disorders were also significant influences on the development of psychoanalytic object relations theory. Mayman (1976)

responseSet.title: Subjectivity, Intentionality, and the Emergence of Reality Testing in Early Infancy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Subjectivity, Intentionality, and the Emergence of Reality Testing in Early Infancy

Thomas Horner PHD

Thomas Horner

Thomas Horner  

The peek-a-boo behavior of a young infant is used to illustrate early processes of intentionality and reality testing. Based on the observations made in this study, as well as findings from similar studies of face-to-face behavior in infants, a general paradigm of cyclical engagement-disengagement of objects on the part of infants is described and discussed. Observations of the transfer of engagement-disengagement behavior on the part of the infant from oral to visual modalities are presented. The observations are discussed from a psychoanalytic object-relational standpoint with integrations of cognitive theory. Implications for an emerging subjectivity in the infant are briefly addressed.

responseSet.title: Discussion of “New Directions in Psychoanalysis” by Michael Franz Basch
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of “New Directions in Psychoanalysis” by Michael Franz Basch

Frank Lachmann PHD

Frank Lachmann

New York

Frank Lachmann  

Additional directions for psychoanalysis are added to the list offered by Dr. Basch. These include the recent studies of the psychology of women, experimental investigations of psychoanalytic propositions, and the empirical infant literature. Some misconceptions of the past are being brushed away so that stereotypical beliefs about women and infants can be challenged as they appear in clinical practice. Furthermore, the empirical infant literature is clarifying both differences and similarities between the mother-infant dyad and the analyst-patient relationship. Analysts informed by these new directions can bring an expanded listening perspective into the therapeutic setting.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Case Presentation: Adolescent Borderline Patient with Severe Cardiac Involvement

Diagnosis and Treatment from a Psychoanalytic-Developmental Perspective

Anita Hurtig PHD

Anita Hurtig

Anita Hurtig  

This paper reviews a case treatment of an 18-year-old female seen in psychotherapy on a weekly basis for one year. The young woman presented a range of problems, including night terrors and fantasies of uncontrolled homicidal behavior. Her early history was noteworthy for severe cardiac anomalies at birth resulting in extensive hospitalizations and surgeries.

Treatment focused on three major areas: (1) the development of a sense of psychological and physical mastery anchored in feelings of self-esteem; (2) the growth of object constancy, trust, and differentiation; and (3) recognition, acceptance, and control of powerful feelings of anger over unmet dependency needs. During her therapy, such symptoms as sleeplessness, suicidal ideation, rage fantasies, and depressed affect, were notably reduced. The underlying borderline personality organization remained basically unchanged; what remained noteworthy was the patient's growing ease with her physical condition, specifically, reduced anxiety over sudden death.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

René A. Spitz: Dialogues from Infancy—Selected Papers

edited by R. N. Emde. New York: International Universities Press, 1983.

Joel Shor PHD

Joel Shor

Joel Shor  

During the 1940s Rene Spitz (1887-1974) seemed a somewhat shadowy figure in American psychoanalysis, while Hartmann, Kris, and Loewenstein were in the forefront of the proceedings. Spitz maintained an elusive, marginal presence also at conferences of the American Psychological Association and of the emerging organizations for child development and mental health. He was one of the few researchers then primarily reporting direct observations on infants; now the fresh concern with character disorders has spurred much research in this area together with respect for its findings. Today, a decade since his death, his central ideas about “dialogue” from infancy may serve comprehensively to encompass the major new trends in clinical psychoanalysis as well as in developmental theory.

This substantial volume includes his papers from 1945 to 1972. Most famous now are his early studies on deprivation of mothering, “Hospitalism,” “Analytic Depression,” and “Autoerotism.” His later formulations, beyond both traditional object relations theory and primary narcissism, yield a fresh perspective and a possible framework for the current polarization of psychoanalytic concerns with object relations theories and self psychologies. His final hypotheses also evoke old, unsettled philosophical issues about the nature-nurture controversy and the question of an original self, a human soul. However, I intend to venture no further than into some implications of Spitz's work on dialogue for a rethinking of our classical, clinical approach to analytic patients.

Requests for reprints should be sent to Joel Shor, 1300 Tigertail Road, Los Angeles, CA 90049.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Effects of Subliminal Symbiotic Gratification and the Magic of Believing on Achievement

Harold Cook PHD

Harold Cook

Harold Cook  

The present experiment examined the effect of a subliminally presented symbiotic gratification and a magic of believing message on academic achievement. Graduate students were randomly assigned to one of the two experiemental message conditions, or to a control message condition, and received on the average 12 sessions, 10 exposurers per session, of 4-msec visual subliminal presentation of one of the three messages. Each session occurred immediately prior to a lecture in either a statistics or a measurement class. Each of the courses was taught in a traditional manner by the regular faculty, who were naive regarding the experimental conditions. Objective final examinations for each course revealed statistically significant differences in favor of the symbiotic gratification experimental condition over the control condition. No differences were obtained between the symbiotic and magic of believing conditions. Research and clinical implications of the findings are also explored.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Foundations of Psychoanalysis: A Philosophical Critique

Adolf Grünbaum. Berkeley, CA: University of California Press, 1984

Abraham Edel PHD

Abraham Edel

Abraham Edel  

This is a work of importance in the current, apparently ever-widening, debates about the “scienticity” of psychoanalysis. Professor Grunbaum is an eminent philosopher of science, particularly of physics. He is thoroughly steeped in the literature of psychoanalysis, new as well as old, and does not rest his account merely on the Freudian corpus. Moreover, he is strictly judicial in his critique; if occasionally he allows himself a barbed comment on an interpreter, it is more in the tone of a judge rebuking a witness to underscore the standards from which the latter has departed.

These qualities are evident in the strategy of the book. Grünbaum makes it clear that the inquiry moves toward a verdict of unproven with respect to the scientific claims of psychoanalytic clinical theory, perhaps even the stronger verdict of unprovable in the terms in which it is traditionally cast. Yet he is not hospitable to the promiscuous reconstructions that set psychoanalysis apart from the mainstream of scientific endeavor, whether on subjectivist or phenomenological or hermeneutical grounds. As he sees it, Freud rightly claimed that psychoanalysis was to be judged as a science in its study of human processes. Grünbaum has the highest respect for Freud's stature as a scientific methodologist.

Requests for reprints should be sent to Abraham Edel, Ph.D., Research Professor, Department of Philosophy, University of Pennsylvania, 305 Logan Hall, Philadelphia, PA 19104.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic and Biological Perspectives on Depression: Contradictory or Complementary?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic and Biological Perspectives on Depression

Contradictory or Complementary?

Dewey Cornell PHD

Dewey Cornell

Ann Arbor, MI

Dewey Cornell  

The emergence of biological psychiatry as a major mental health movement and theoretical point of view presents important issues for psychoanalytic psychology in conceptualizing and treating psychopathology. Psychoanalytic and biological perspectives on depression, as well as other disorders, are often seen as representing contradictory and mutually exclusive theoretical models. This paper critically reviews some of the primary research evidence for the biological psychiatric diagnosis of endogenous depression, and challenges the tendency to construe findings as a basis for discounting the role of psychogenic factors or the value of psychological treatment approaches. Brief attention is given to philosophical issues of reductionism and mind-body dualism, which pose serious conceptual problems for a biological model of psychopathology. The potential for viewing biological and psychological factors as complementary is considered. Freud's early effort to devise a psychogenic model of psychopathology recognized this potential and is reflected in his attempt to build a metapsychological bridge between physiological and mental phenomena. Psychoanalytic theory may provide the essential framework for incorporating both biological and psychological perspectives on depression.

responseSet.title: Infantile Narcissism and the Active Infant
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Infantile Narcissism and the Active Infant**

Irene Fast PHD

Irene Fast

Irene Fast  

Recent proposals to discard the concept of infantile narcissism as incompatible with currently available observations of infants pose a dilemma for psychoanalytic psychology because that concept has been of major importance in concurrent clinical investigations of borderline disorders. A reformulation of Freud's theory of infantile narcissism is proposed, based on Piaget's model of child development. It proposes that objectively the child is actively engaged with its environment from the beginning. Subjectively, however, in its own understanding, the infant does not recognize the world as external. Major phenomena of infantile narcissism are entailed by this model. Clinical illustrations are used to show its implications for the observed phenomena of borderline disorder in adults and children.

responseSet.title: The Use of Fantasy in Borderline Disorders of Childhood: A Case Presentation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Use of Fantasy in Borderline Disorders of Childhood

A Case Presentation

Nell Logan PHD

Nell Logan

Nell Logan  

The theoretical and clinical literature on children and adolescents has presented rich and detailed descriptions of a group of children and adolescents who show characteristics similar to each other but different from those children and adolescents who show the characteristics associated with the psychoses and neuroses (Anthony & Gilpin, 1981; Chethik, 1979; Ekstein, 1966; Masterson, 1972; Petti & Law, 1982; Rinsley, 1980; Robson, 1983.) Terms such as borderline child, borderline disorder, and borderline personality disorder have been used to distinguish these children and adolescents. Various aspects of treatment with these children and adolescents have also been discussed. This literature, however, has not focused in much depth on the course of the lengthy treatments that may occur.

This paper presents the initial assessment and the course of 8 years of therapy with a “borderline child.” This treatment focused especially on the use of fantasy in understanding, responding to, and working on psychological issues.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Patterns of Ego Boundary Disturbance in Neurotic, Borderline, and Schizophrenic Patients

Howard Lerner PHD , Alan Sugarman PHD and Carol Barbour PHD

Carol Barbour

Ann Arbor, Michigan

Ann Arbor, Michigan

Carol Barbour  

As part of a series of articles addressing unresolved conceptual and empirical questions regarding structural diagnosis and the borderline concept, this study examines differences in the representation of interpersonal boundaries among neurotic, borderline, and schizophrenic patients. Drawing on the contributions of Blatt and his colleagues, a Boundary Disturbance Scale was constructed by applying a developmental continuum of boundary representation to a traditional ego psychological analysis of thought disorder on the Rorschach. The results of the study support the hypothesis that severe borderline psychopathology can be conceptualized as a failure to maintain the inner–outer boundary, that schizophrenia involves a deficit in maintaining the distinction between self and others, and that meaningful developmental distinctions between diagnostic groups can be made on the basis of the Rorschach. The findings demonstrate the broad explanatory scope gained by a developmental object relations model in clarifying perplexing diagnostic and conceptual issues involving borderline phenomena on the Rorschach, specifically, (1) the representation of boundaries, (2) the cognitive disturbance involving the inner–outer boundary, and (3) the nature of the short–lived, circumscribed psychotic episodes in these patients.

responseSet.title: The Role of Affect in Cognitive Psychology and Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role of Affect in Cognitive Psychology and Psychoanalysis

Allan Rosenblatt MD

Allan Rosenblatt

Allan Rosenblatt  

Neither cognitive psychology nor psychoanalysis has developed a comprehensive theory of affects. Such a theory of affect, integrating conceptions derived from Arnold, Lazarus, Tornkins, Bowlby, and Langer, is outlined. Affect is viewed as the subjective experience of feed back appraisal processes operating as part of motivational systems. Quality and intensity of affect are determined by the characteristics of the underlying motivational system and by the degree, direction, and rate of change of mismatch. Examples of motivational systems and their associated affects are given and the role of affect in cognitive development, as well as perception, are considered. By examples, the clinical application of these concepts is shown to avoid false leads and permit development of more effective clinical approaches.

responseSet.title: The Current Status of Psychoanalytic Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Current Status of Psychoanalytic Theory

Robert Holt PHD

Robert Holt

Robert Holt  

In recent years, metapsychology has been effectively destroyed by a series of critiques, here summarized. Clinical psychoanalysis, its heart, is a testable scientific theory and need not be trivialized by being reduced to a hermeneutics, but it has been exposed by Grunbaum and Rubinstein as scriously lacking in empirical verification. Its genetic hypotheses are extremely difficult to test; the clinical case study is useful only as a means of generating hypotheses. As Rubinstein has shown, however, the clinical theory can be systematized and stated in probabilistic propositions testable by statistical research. Its fundamental propositions can be tested only by nonpsychoanalytic data, however. Object relations and self psychology have had a large vogue but do not address the fundamental theoretical problems. Those threaten the survival of psychoanalysis, but are being complacently ignored. Some possible solutions are discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

Do I Dare Disturb the Universe? A Memorial to Wilfred R. Bion

James S. Grotstein. Beverly Hills, CA: Caesura Press, 1981, 673 pp., $19.95.

W. Meissner SJ MD

W. Meissner

W. Meissner  

The present volume is a rather hefty memorial to the late Wilfred Bion, and it seems to fulfill its objectives admirably. Most of the well-known Kleinians, or post-Kleinians, are represented here, some quite heavily, some even substantively.

To open the cover of this compendious volume is to enter a Bionic world that seems somewhat strange and even befuddling to the average American-born and raised psychoanalyst. There is no primer here in Kleinian or Bionic rudiments. It is heavy-going, heady stuff, at times almost too heady for reading. It calls for a kind of lectio meditativa of more scholarly days. In view of the complexity and the expanse of the material, I will content myself with offering some general impressions and adding a few comments about particular contributions.

Perhaps the primary impression I derived from my reading was of the extent to which the roots of psychoanalysis intertwine with philosophical assumptions and perspectives. The penetrating manner in which Bion extended some of the basic Kleinian insights makes clear the extent to which the Kleinian-Bionic approach to the understanding of the mind is rooted in Kantian and ultimately Platonic approaches. I have never before had so vivid an impression of the Kleinian thought as—in some fundamental philosophical sense—pre-experiential. Like Platonic ideas or the Kantian synthetic a priori, this aspect of Bion's thinking is underlined in his notion of innate preconceptions and the essential role they play in human thought processes.

A second impression is of the vast difference between Kleinian and Bionic methodology and the more empirical, inductive, and theoretical emphases of American psychoanalysis. The prototype of the American approach to psychoanalytic matters is the ego psychology of Hartmann, Kris, and Rapaport. There the model is scientific, and the emphasis falls on the understanding of

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of Transformation in Psychoanalysis

Norbert Freedman PHD

Norbert Freedman

Norbert Freedman  

Transformation is viewed as the essential process of structure building. This article delineates levels of meaning organization that undergo alteration and move increasingly toward higher levels of symbolization. The transition from each stage of mental organization involves a dialectic conflict leading to recorganization at a higher level. This general thesis is illustrated by clinical material, reference to the literature, and a body of research data.

responseSet.title: Interpersonal Phenomena in the Rorschachs of Extremely Feminine Boys
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Interpersonal Phenomena in the Rorschachs of Extremely Feminine Boys

Steven Tuber PHD and Susan Coates PHD

Susan Coates

Susan Coates  

The Rorschachs of 14 feminine boys were intensively studied. Relative to the records of normal subjects, feminine boys displayed an inordinate investment in quasi-human at the expense of fully human responses. When percepts had individuals in interaction, the preponderance of these interactions were malevolent. Boundary disturbances, as indicated by lapses of thought organization, were commonplace. Females were frequently represented in idealized, stereotypical, or caricatured ways. Lastly, overt gender confusion was seen in single percepts of humans. It is proposed that these results provide evidence of severely disturbed internalized object representations. As such, boyhood feminity is inextricably linked to psychopathology and not a secondary consequence of peer rejection, as has been argued by others.

[[RunningHead]]

Transitional Relatedness and Borderline Personality Disorder

Steven Cooper PHD , Christopher Perry MD , Lizbeth Hoke MA and Nancy Richman MED

Nancy Richman

Nancy Richman  

This study examined various aspects of transitional relatedness among individuals diagnosed as borderline or antisocial personality disorder. This study was a follow–up to an earlier report (Cooper, 1983) using the Rorschach Transitional Object Scale, which found only marginal support for Modell's clinical hypothesis that the borderline personality disorder involves a developmental arrest at the stage of the transitional object. The authors approached the concept of transitional relatedness as a series of external and internalized modes of relating including the perceptual capacity to distinguish between object and symbol on the Rorschach as well as self–reported current and past reliance on transitional objects and self–soothing activities. The study failed to provide evidence to suggest that reliance on a past or present transitional object per se is a unique or diagnostic feature of the borderline personality disorder. Particular aspects of current transitional relatedness, however, were strongly associated with borderline psychopathology. The clinical and research implications of these findings are discussed.

responseSet.title: The Impact of Maternal Personality on Individuation During Adolescence
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Impact of Maternal Personality on Individuation During Adolescence

David Brandt PHD and Helen Silverman PHD

Helen Silverman

New York

Helen Silverman  

The role of the mother in the resolution of the rapprochement crisis as it continues into adolescence is examined through the analyses of a 14-year-old boy and his mother. The symptoms of the adolescent were anxiety, poor school performance, and drug abuse. The mother sought treatment for intense anxiety and overwhelming dread when contemplating her future and that of her son. The dynamics of the mother, the nature of the symbiotic bond, and their impact on the inability of the adolescent to effectively disengage from anaclitic parental ties are discussed. The importance of understanding parental dynamics in the analysis of adolescents is stressed from theoretical and clinical viewpoints.

responseSet.title: Narcissism and Borderline States: Kernberg, Kohut, and Psychotherapy
responseSet.authorMast: Marc Tonkin and  Harold J. Fine
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narcissism and Borderline States

Kernberg, Kohut, and Psychotherapy

Marc Tonkin PHD and Harold Fine PHD

Harold Fine

Harold Fine  

The post-Freudian era of consciousness is examined, wherein the individual is reconsidered from two viewpoints—narcissism and borderline states—via two seminal thinkers, Kohut and Kernberg. This is elaborated on through discussion of a “conflict” in Freud's work (i.e., the paradoxical conflicts in Freud between personal insight and natural science metaphors) and the utilization of paradigm shift and crisis in science. This continues in the dialectic concerning the theoretical and treatment styles of Kohut and Kernberg. The advantages, liabilities, and linguistic structures of both writers are studied in relation to their explorations of disturbances of the self.

responseSet.title: Transference and the Human Condition: A Cognitive–Affective Perspective
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference and the Human Condition

A Cognitive–Affective Perspective

Jerome Singer PHD

Jerome Singer

Jerome Singer  

The transference phenomenon is reexamined from the perspective of its implications as a general feature of human experience, its specific role in psychoanalysis as a manifestation of a transference neurosis, and (in the light of differing approaches to transference interpretation) its role in the personality change process of psychoanalysis. The convergence between social cognitive concepts of personal constructs, schemata, prototypes, scripts, and nuclear scenes and the methods used for identifying transference from psychoanalytic protocols is noted. Transference is interpreted as an inherent feature of the perspective of the human being as an information-seeking, information-processing organism whose experience and expression of emotions is tied to the novelty and complexity of such a continuing cognitive process.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

New Directions in Psychoanalysis

Michael Basch MD

Michael Basch

Chicago

Michael Basch  

A number of significant developments augur well for the future of psychoanalysis, both as a method of therapy and as a branch of psychological science. The contributions of Heinz Kohut, together with the explanations for his clinical findings as made possible by developmental psychology, cybernetics, communication science, and neurophysiology are reviewed in the context of their significance for Freud's pioneering discoveries.

responseSet.title: Freud, Father, and Mother: Quantitative Aspects
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud, Father, and Mother

Quantitative Aspects

George Mahl PHD

George Mahl

George Mahl  

The frequency with which Freud used father and mother in his psychological writings was investigated. Overall, he used father much more frequently than mother. This difference began with those writings that followed his father's death and his self-analysis. A most striking, though transitory, reversal followed immediately upon the death of his mother. The data suggest that Freud blended objective, scientific, and sublimated, personal issues in his parental psychology. An ancillary result shows the yearly creation of the pages of his psychological writings.
